doc.title: Cash dividends — declaration to payment
doc.event: Pace Corp.'s board declares a $1-per-share cash dividend on its 50,000 outstanding shares. Prepare the entries for the declaration, record, and payment dates.

variants.label: $50,000 declared out of ample retained earnings

entries.caption: Declaration date — the liability is born

lines.why: Declaration is the legal commitment — retained earnings shrinks the moment the board votes, not when cash moves.
lines.trap: Dividends are NEVER an expense. They're a distribution of earnings, so they hit Retained Earnings directly (or a Dividends account closed into it).

lines.label: a real current liability
lines.why: Once declared, it's enforceable debt — a current liability until paid.
lines.trap: UNdeclared dividends (even preferred arrears) are never a liability. Declaration is the switch.

entries.caption: Payment date — settle the liability (record date in between: NO ENTRY)

lines.label: clear it
lines.why: The liability created at declaration is settled.
lines.trap: The RECORD date between these two entries gets NO journal entry — it only determines WHO gets the checks. Writing an entry there is a classic exam giveaway.

lines.label: checks out
lines.why: Cash finally moves — the last of the three dates.
lines.trap: Retained Earnings already took its hit at declaration; touching it again here double-counts.

variants.label: $50,000 declared but only $30,000 of retained earnings exists

entries.caption: Declaration — the excess over earnings is a return OF capital

lines.label: the earnings portion
lines.why: Only $30,000 of accumulated earnings exists to distribute — that's all the true 'dividend' there is.
lines.trap: Retained Earnings cannot go negative from a dividend — the account caps the earnings portion.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.label: the liquidating portion
lines.why: The other $20,000 hands shareholders back their OWN contributed capital — a liquidating dividend, debited to paid-in capital.
lines.trap: Return OF capital, not return ON capital — shareholders must be told, because for them it's not income either.

lines.label: the full declaration
lines.why: The liability is the whole $50,000 regardless of which equity accounts funded it.
lines.trap: The split happens on the DEBIT side only — the payable and the eventual cash don't care.

computationPaths.narration: Liquidating split: dividend declared 50,000 − retained earnings available 30,000 = 20,000 of contributed capital going back out the door.

memorize.body: Three dates, two entries: DECLARE (RE down, payable up) → RECORD (no entry — just the shareholder list) → PAY (payable down, cash down).
Dividends are declared on OUTSTANDING shares only — treasury shares never collect. Outstanding = issued − treasury; using 'issued' overstates the dividend.
Liquidating portion = dividend declared − retained earnings available. That slice debits paid-in capital, not RE.

questions.prompt: A cash dividend has been declared. What journal entry is made on the DATE OF RECORD?

distractors.feedback: No entry at all on the record date — it only fixes WHO receives payment. The liability was born at declaration; cash moves at payment. The record date is bookkeeping-silent.

accountBank.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common

build.scaffold: Walk the three dates in order and remember the middle one is silent. Declaration debits equity (RE first, PIC only for any liquidating excess) and births the payable; payment just swaps the payable for cash.
